request.question: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?

title: Eli Jaxon-Bear
Hervey Alan
Jan van der Watt
Peter Bos
Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)
Der Bärenhäuter (opera)
Christopher Larkin (conductor)
Rick van der Linden
Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland
Derek Hammond-Stroud
sentences: Eli Jaxon-Bear (born Elliot J. Zeldow in 1947) is an American spiritual teacher and author.
He lives in Ashland, Oregon with his wife, fellow spiritual teacher Gangaji.
Before he met his teacher, Sri H.W.L. Poonja, in 1990, he was best known for his work on the spiritual dimension of the Enneagram.
Following his meeting with Poonjaji he continued to teach, carrying his teacher’s message to the West.
Hervey Alan (22 February 1910 – 12 January 1982) was an English operatic bass and voice teacher.

During his career he sang leading roles with most of Great Britain's major opera institutions, including the Edinburgh Festival, the Glyndebourne Festival, the Royal Opera House, the Sadler's Wells Opera, and the Welsh National Opera.
He is best known for creating the role of Mr. Redburn in the world premiere of Benjamin Britten's "Billy Budd" at the Royal Opera House, London, on 1 December 1951.
Music critic Elizabeth Forbes wrote that his voice was "dark toned, resonant", and "especially effective as Zaccaria in "Nabucco".

Jan Gabriël Van der Watt (born 5 November 1952) is a South African biblical scholar and Bible translator who moved to the Netherlands in 2009 to take up a chair in New Testament and Source texts of early Christianity at Radboud University in Nijmegen.

For a quarter of a century previously, he was professor at the University of Pretoria, where he was named as one of the 100 most influential academic thinkers in the 100-year history of the University of Pretoria, South Africa (see www.up.ac.za under Leading Minds).

Van der Watt was also rated as international acknowledged researcher that is regarded by some of his international peers as international leader in his field, (see www.nrf.ac.za under rated researchers).
Van der Watt is internationally best known for his monograph: Family of the King: Dynamics of Metaphor in the Gosepl According to John.

Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.
He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.
Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".
Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.
As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Christopher Larkin is an American conductor who is best known for his work within the field of opera.
He was Associate Conductor of the Houston Grand Opera for a number of years where he notably conducted the world premieres of Mark Adamo's "Little Women" and Michael Daugherty’s "Jackie O".
He is also the former Music Director of the New York City Opera National Company.

As a guest conductor, Larkin has worked for numerous opera houses including Wexford Festival Opera, Santa Fe Opera, Washington National Opera, Portland Opera, Florentine Opera, Opera Pacific, Opera Carolina, and Boston Lyric Opera among many others.
He has also worked as an Assistant Conductor and vocal coach for the Metropolitan Opera, San Francisco Opera, Opera Company of Philadelphia, Vancouver Opera, and the Canadian Opera Company.
Rick van der Linden (5 August 1946, Badhoevedorp, North Holland - 22 January 2006, Groningen) was a Dutch composer and keyboardist.
Van der Linden first gained fame as a member of Ekseption, but he played in several other bands including most notably Trace, as well as solo.
Van der Linden was best known for his reworkings of classical music in a pop music domain, often with jazz improvisations.

"Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland " (original: "Nu kom der Heyden heyland ", English: "Now come, Saviour of the heathens") is a Lutheran chorale of 1524 with words written by Martin Luther, based on "Veni redemptor gentium " by Ambrose.
It was printed in the Erfurt "Enchiridion" of 1524.
The chorale was used as the prominent hymn for the first Sunday of Advent for centuries.

It was used widely in organ settings by Protestant baroque composers, most notably Johann Sebastian Bach: he set it as the opening chorale prelude BWV 599 of Orgelbüchlein; and three times—as BWV 659 (one of his best known organ compositions), BWV 660 and BWV 661—in his Great Eighteen Chorale Preludes.

Bach used the hymn in his chorale cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 62 (1724) and in the opening chorale fantasia of his earlier cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 61 , BWV 61 (1714).
Max Reger composed a chorale prelude as No. 29 of his 52 Chorale Preludes, Op. 67 in 1902.

Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".
He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must be followed exactly):
1) Output format: return a single string in the `summary` field. That string must be a compact, structured summary containing these three labeled sections in this exact order and spelling:  
   First-hop evidence:  
   Bridge needed:  
   Retrieval direction:  
Each section should be short (each 1–3 short clauses or sentences) and the whole summary should be concise.

2) First-hop evidence: extract and report the concrete fact(s) present in the provided `passages` that are relevant to the question. Name the passage title(s) you used and give a very short quoted phrase or paraphrase (<=15 words) showing the supporting fact. Preserve the concise factual style (e.g., "Alan Ruck — born July 1, 1956" or "Passage: 'Ferris Bueller' cast lists Alan Ruck"). Do not invent facts.

3) Bridge needed: explicitly state the single overlap, entity, or missing fact that the next retrieval hop must verify to answer the question (for example: the person to check for cast membership; the named tournament page to fetch; the album title whose release year is needed). If the passages already name a candidate title or person, you may cite that candidate here as the bridge to be verified, but do not treat or phrase it as the final answer.

4) Retrieval direction: give ordered, concrete next retrieval actions (1., 2., ...) or one-line query templates the next agent should use to verify the bridge and obtain the missing fact. Examples: fetch the page titled exactly "<Tournament Title>" and inspect its 'Venues'/'Schedule' sections; retrieve the film/TV cast list for "<Work>"; search "<Person name>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org. Keep steps explicit and in order. Do not ask for external sources beyond retrieving the named pages or running the stated queries.

5) Prohibitions and constraints: do NOT produce a final answer to the question or assert that the bridge overlap is confirmed. Do NOT invent or infer missing facts. Avoid long verbatim excerpts from sources (keep quotes <=15 words). If a passage supports a short candidate label (a single title or name), do not let that stand alone as the summary — still include the three labeled sections and a retrieval plan. Keep language neutral and factual. 

6) When to include additional guidance (examples of minimal behaviors to follow):
   - If a passage names a tournament/work that appears to be the bridge, put that exact title in 'Bridge needed:' and instruct fetching that page and which sections (Venues/Matches/Schedule/Cast) to check. 
   - If the passages identify a person (e.g., an assassin or credited actor) as the bridge, name that person in 'Bridge needed:' and provide a short retrieval query pattern (e.g., '"<Name>" birthplace site:wikipedia.org' or 'fetch "<Name>" biography').
   - If a passage lists some but not all of a question's film credits for an actor, state which film titles from the question are confirmed in 'First-hop evidence:' and which are unverified in 'Bridge needed:', then give queries to fetch the missing film/actor pages.

7) Preserve concise, passage-grounded evidence extraction and always make the next-hop actionable. The `summary` must not change the input/output field names.

Return only the `summary` string following the rules above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Keep the native I/O exactly: return only the single `summary` string field. Work only from facts present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`. Do not add information that is not present in those inputs.

General integration rule (default): If the `context` asserts a factual claim and one or more `passages` corroborate that claim, the `summary` must (a) restate the claim succinctly, (b) name at least one supporting passage by its title, and (c) include at most one very short confirming fragment or brief quoted clause taken directly from that passage (no long verbatim spans). Keep wording concise (usually 1–3 short sentences) and preserve factual tokens exactly as they appear in the inputs when reporting names, numbers, or titles.

Formatting and conditional rules (apply only when their triggers are met):
- Entity-label answers (clear single candidate): If the question requests identification of a named entity (person, organization, place, institution) and the passages support a single clear candidate, output a terse label: only the canonical name as it appears in the sources (1–6 words), with no surrounding sentence framing or trailing punctuation. If multiple clear candidates exist, output a concise comma-separated list of names (still without sentence framing).
- Bare actor/casting answers: If the question asks "who" about casting (contains 'who' and 'stars' or 'as'/'as the'), output only the actor's name or the shortest noun phrase (≤ ~4 words) drawn from the inputs. Do not produce a full sentence or explanatory text.
- Role-confirmation outputs (identity + role): If the passages supply both (A) a passage linking a named person to the subject (e.g., identifies someone as a game's official spokesperson or cover person) and (B) a passage giving that person's occupation/alias, produce 1–3 concise sentences: (1) briefly restate the claim from `context`; (2) add two micro-evidence clauses, each a short paraphrase with the passage title in parentheses (one for the subject-game passage, one for the biographical passage); (3) finish with one integrated sentence linking the person's full name/alias, occupation, and the stated role. Keep paraphrases short and do not invent facts. End with an explicit short retention note only when the downstream pipeline needs the role preserved (e.g., 'Retain this fact for final-answer agent.').
- Comparative nationality or similar yes/no-by-comparison questions: If the question asks whether two people share a nationality (or similar binary comparison), do not start the `summary` with a standalone 'Yes.' or 'No.' Instead produce a single declarative sentence that names the first person and their nationality, the second person and their nationality, and then the conclusion about whether they share nationality. If identity is ambiguous from the passages, state that ambiguity briefly and what is missing rather than asserting a conclusion.
- Multi-passage bookkeeping (explicit-support vs metadata-only): When `context` asserts a relationship and `passages` include at least one passage that explicitly states the relationship and other passages that only supply metadata (title, year, director, offices/reign, dynasty description, etc.), the `summary` should: (a) paraphrase the first-hop assertion in one brief clause; (b) name the passage(s) that explicitly support the assertion and give a one-line paraphrase or very short quote from them; (c) list any metadata-only passage(s) with the specific metadata to be carried forward (title, year, director, office, reign dates, dynasty name) in one line each; and (d) conclude with a concise integration sentence indicating which passage provides explicit support and which provide metadata. Do not attribute a link to a passage unless that link is present there.
- Numeric/chart and definitional integration: If the question asks for a chart position and the inputs include (a) a passage naming the song and its chart peak and (b) a passage defining the chart (e.g., that it is the country's main music sales chart), produce 1–2 concise sentences that (1) name the song and artist, (2) state the numeric peak and chart name exactly as given, and (3) add a short clarifying phrase that the named chart is the country's main music sales chart. Keep wording compact and grounded in the passages.

Grounding, quoting, and conservatism: Always cite supporting passage titles when they corroborate a `context` claim. Include at most one very short quoted fragment or a brief confirming clause from a cited passage; do not include long verbatim quotations. Preserve exact tokens from the inputs when reporting names, numbers, titles, or aliases. If the passages resolve identity ambiguity, present the resolved identification directly (without hedging); if identity is unresolved, explicitly note the ambiguity and what evidence is missing.

Prohibitions (hard constraints):
- Do not invent facts or expand beyond the provided inputs.
- Do not output fields other than `summary`.
- Do not begin the `summary` with a single-word answer token such as 'Yes.' or 'No.'.
- Do not produce long verbatim spans from passages; use only very short quoted fragments when needed.
- Do not convert concise entity-label answers into full-sentence conversational text unless a conditional rule above requires a short integrated sentence (e.g., role-confirmation).
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only that minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional narrowly scoped, evidence-driven adjustments (apply only when their listed triggers match):

A. Strip trailing parenthetical disambiguators when safe
- Trigger: If summary_1 and summary_2 exactly agree (case-insensitive for agreement detection) on the same short named-entity string and that shared string ends with a single trailing parenthetical disambiguator of the form ' ( ... )' at the end of the string.
- Behavior: Remove that single final parenthetical block and any adjacent whitespace/punctuation, and emit the remaining base name verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization) as the minimal answer inside [[ ## answer ## ]].
- Safety/fallback: Only perform this stripping when both summaries exactly match the full phrase and when removing the parenthetical would not produce an empty or clearly ambiguous result. If removal would produce an empty or clearly ambiguous result, or if you cannot determine safety, emit the original agreed phrase (including the parenthetical) instead.
- Prohibition: Do not append any new parenthetical, acronym, or qualifier to the emitted base name; do not add surrounding punctuation.

B. Do not append qualifiers or parentheticals
- Trigger: Whenever selecting a short minimal answer substring from the summaries (1–5 words) for emission.
- Behavior: Emit exactly the substring as it appears in the chosen summary (normalized by trimming surrounding whitespace/punctuation) and do not append additional tokens, parenthetical qualifiers, demonyms, type labels, or metadata. If the candidate answer would add any words, punctuation, or parenthetical text not present verbatim in the selected substring, remove those additions and emit only the exact substring.
- This rule forbids adding trailing parenthetical disambiguators or nationality tags unless that exact text is already the chosen minimal substring.

C. Parenthetical-content preference for fuller canonical names (conservative)
- Trigger: Both summaries contain the same short named-entity substring that matches the pattern 'X (Y)' (some text immediately followed by parentheses), and the parenthetical content Y contains more than one token and appears to be a fuller/canonical name.
- Behavior: You may extract and emit the parenthetical text Y (stripped of enclosing parentheses and surrounding whitespace) as the minimal answer, preserving original characters and capitalization.
- Safety constraints: Only prefer the parenthetical content when all of the following hold: (1) both summaries present the same 'X (Y)' phrase; (2) Y contains more tokens than X (i.e., is demonstrably longer); (3) Y contains alphabetic characters (is not primarily a numeric date or numeric code); and (4) preferring Y would not conflict with any other stricter rule below (for example, do not prefer Y if X is a 4-digit year that should remain a year answer). If these conditions are not met, fall back to emitting the agreed phrase (applying the trailing-parenthetical stripping rule A if appropriate).
- Do not emit the combined 'X (Y)' form; emit either Y (when allowed) or the safe base form.

D. Years and adjacent parentheticals
- Trigger: If a visible summary contains a 4-digit year token immediately followed by a parenthetical clause in the same token sequence (pattern: 'YYYY ( ... )').
- Behavior: Prefer emitting the 4-digit year as the minimal answer unless the parenthetical is a concise clarifying phrase that is explicitly the requested answer by the question and is short (roughly 2–7 words). Do not replace a clear year answer with a longer multi-word parenthetical unless the question specifically asks for that clarifier and the summaries unambiguously present the parenthetical as the requested minimal fact.
- This preserves concise-date answers and avoids replacing years with explanatory clauses unless clearly warranted by the question and the summaries.

E. 'Have in common' singularization
- Trigger: The question contains wording like 'have in common' or 'in common' referring to two named people, and both summaries contain the same short plural noun phrase (case-insensitive match) describing the shared property.
- Behavior: Emit the singular form of the agreed plural noun as the minimal answer token. Apply simple regular morphological conversions only (handle common cases such as removing a trailing 's', converting trailing 'ies' -> 'y', or stripping a trailing 'es' when appropriate). Preserve the original capitalization from the summaries. Do not consult external facts for this transformation; this is purely a morphological normalization on the confirmed shared phrase.

F. Conservative diminutive expansion for person names
- Trigger: Summary_1 and summary_2 satisfy rule 2 (they contain the same short person-name string) and the given-name token in that shared string is a common diminutive/nickname (e.g., 'Don').
- Behavior: If you have high internal confidence that the diminutive corresponds to a specific canonical full given name, you may emit that full given name combined with the same surname as the summaries (include a middle name only if you have high internal confidence). If you do not have high internal confidence, emit the short name verbatim per rule 2.
- Constraint: This expansion is optional and conservative; do not invent or append middle names unless highly confident. Do not add roles, dates, or descriptors.

G. When visible summaries already agree, do not ask instead of emitting
- Trigger: Summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named token or short phrase (1–5 words) that is a suitable minimal answer.
- Behavior: Do not emit a clarification question in place of the agreed minimal answer. Emit that common short phrase verbatim (preserving capitalization shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]].

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?
hop1_docs: Der Bärenhäuter (opera) | Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.  As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Der Bärenhäuter | Der Bärenhäuter (German "Bearskin man") may refer to:
Bearskin (film) | Der Bärenhäuter is a German movie from the year 1986, based on the fairy tale "Bearskin".

Peter Bos | Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.  He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.

Der Prozeß (opera) | Der Prozeß ("English: The Trial ") is a German-language opera in two parts, divided into nine scenes, with music by Gottfried von Einem and a libretto by Boris Blacher and , based on the novel by Franz Kafka.  Composed over the period 1950 to 1952, this was von Einem's second opera.  He dedicated it to the psychologist and theologian Oskar Pfister, who had been his therapist, and to his former teacher, Karl Christian Jensen.

Derek Hammond-Stroud | Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".  He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

Pomona (opera) | Der Sieg der fruchtbaren Pomona (The Victory of Fruitful Pomona) is a German-language opera by Reinhard Keiser to a libretto by Christian Heinrich Postel which premiered at the Hamburg Opera on 19 October 1702, for the birthday of Frederick IV of Denmark.  The plot was based on the legend of Pomona and Jupiter, and the libretto was the last of a series which the elderly poet Postel had provided to the young Keiser during the five previous opera seasons.  The opera was the first of four Keiser operas dedicated to the Danish crown.
response.summary_1:
First-hop evidence: Passage [1] — "Der Bärenhäuter ... by Siegfried Wagner" and Passage [1] — "influenced by his teacher Humperdinck."  
Bridge needed: Engelbert Humperdinck (the teacher "Humperdinck") — identify which opera he is best known for.  
Retrieval direction: 1. Fetch the page titled "Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)" and inspect the lead/summary and 'Works'/'Notable works' for a statement naming his best-known opera. 2. hop2_docs: Hansel and Gretel (opera) | Hansel and Gretel (German: Hänsel und Gretel ) is an opera by nineteenth-century composer Engelbert Humperdinck, who described it as a "Märchenoper " (fairy-tale opera).  The libretto was written by Humperdinck's sister, Adelheid Wette, based on the Grimm brothers' fairy tale "Hansel and Gretel".  It is much admired for its folk music-inspired themes, one of the most famous being the "Abendsegen " ("Evening Benediction") from act 2.
Engelbert Humperdinck (composer) | Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".

Brother, Come and Dance with Me | "Brother, Come And Dance With Me" is a popular children’s song that was originally written by the German composer Engelbert Humperdinck for his famous opera, "Hänsel und Gretel" (ca. 1891).

Hansel and Gretel | "Hansel and Gretel" (also known as "Hansel and Grettel", "Hansel and Grethel", or "Little Brother and Little Sister") ( or and ; German: Hänsel und Gretel ] ) is a well-known fairy tale of German origin, recorded by the Brothers Grimm and published in 1812.  Hansel and Gretel are a young brother and sister kidnapped by a cannibalistic witch living deep in the forest in a house constructed of cake and confectionery.  The two children escape with their lives by outwitting her.  The tale has been adapted to various media, most notably the opera "Hänsel und Gretel" (1893) by Engelbert Humperdinck.  Under the Aarne–Thompson classification system, "Hansel and Gretel" is classified under Class 327.

Norman Kelley | Norman Kelley (August 27, 1911 – September 4, 2006) was an American operatic tenor who had an active international career during the 1940s through the 1970s.  He was notably a regular performer at the Metropolitan Opera between 1957 and 1961, and he sang in several world premieres with the New York City Opera.  He also notably translated Engelbert Humperdinck's "Hänsel und Gretel" into English, a version first performed in 1967 and used by opera companies to this present day.

List of Colombian writers | This List of Colombian writers is an alphabetical list of writers born or brought up in Colombia, who already have Wikipedia pages in the English or Spanish Wikipedia.  References for information given in the list appear on the Wikipedia pages concerned.  This is a subsidiary list to the List of Colombian people.  The list is far from exhaustive, so please help to expand it by adding Wikipedia page-owning published writers who have written in any genre or field, including science and scholarship, but does not include those whose sole body of work lies outside conventional published literature such as: print journalists, bloggers, editors, librettists, lyricists, songwriters, playwrights, or screenwriters.  Please follow the entry format: use Spanish naming customs by listing both surnames of the person, use "italics" for authors who are best known under a pseudonym, indicate only the year of birth or the years of birth and death, and do not include place of birth or works associated with the writer, as that information should be found in their actual page.
Hansel and Gretel (1963 film) | Hansel and Gretel is a 1963 Australian TV adaptation of the opera "Hansel and Gretel" by Engelbert Humperdinck.
